content.full_text:
October 29, 1976

Diane V. Brown  
International Paper Company  
1620 Eye Street, N.W.  
Suite 700  
Washington D.C. 20006

Dear Ms. Brown:

Pursuant to our telephone conversation of October 27, 1976, I am enclosing materials which may be of assistance to you.

As I stated in that conversation, the International Paper Company political action committee should file a statement of organization with the Secretary of State, Election Division, 111 Capitol Mall, Sacramento, California 95814. In addition, the committee will be required to file a post election campaign statement on or before January 6, 1977. The statement will disclose contributions received and expenditures and contributions made through December 30, 1976. However, if all liabilities have been paid, and no additional contributions are anticipated, the statement may be filed any time after the election and before the deadline.

Under California law, the committee must disclose the name, address, occupation, and employer of each person from whom a contribution of fifty dollars or more has been received. We have not, as yet, devised a simple method for determining when a PAC that makes contributions to federal elections and many state elections must identify contributors. I am enclosing a copy of a letter that was sent to the Santa Fe Railway Company and which describes an appropriate method of apportioning receipts to determine what portion of each contribution is earmarked for California campaigns. In the case of contributions received by the political action committee of International Paper Company, you should determine the percentage of each contribution attributable to California state elections on the basis of the entire amount received by the committee, not the amount transferred to the state and local bank account. For example, suppose the contributions received by the political action committee total $50,000, of which $10,000 is transferred to the state and local bank account, and $1,000 is eventually contributed to California state candidates. The pro rata portion of the contributions

Diane V. Brown  
October 29, 1976  
Page Two

received that is attributable to California state elections would be 1,000/50,000 or 2%, not 1,000/10,000 or 10%. If, on the basis of this calculation, the committee did not receive any contributions from a single source totaling $50 or more, you may file the Association Campaign Statement Short Form (Form 450), instead of the Form 420.

Don't hesitate to contact me if you have any further questions.

Sincerely,

Natalie E. West  
Staff Counsel

NEW:av

Enclosures:
    Form 410
    Form 420
    Form 450
    2 Cal. Adm. Code Sections 18215 and 18225
    Campaign Disclosure Manual
    McKnew letter
    Fact sheet on where to file

sections.question_synthetic: How should International Paper Company's political action committee comply with California campaign finance disclosure requirements, particularly regarding contributor identification thresholds and reporting obligations?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: International Paper Company's PAC must file a statement of organization and post-election campaign statement by January 6, 1977, disclosing contributors of $50 or more based on a pro-rata calculation of California-attributable contributions relative to total PAC receipts, not just amounts transferred to state accounts.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: No formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections present; extracted from body of advice letter. This is a responsive advice letter providing guidance on PAC registration and disclosure obligations. Date is October 29, 1976; document ID 76149.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: How should International Paper Company's political action committee comply with California campaign finance disclosure requirements, particularly regarding contributor identification thresholds and reporting obligations?

CONCLUSION: International Paper Company's PAC must file a statement of organization and post-election campaign statement by January 6, 1977, disclosing contributors of $50 or more based on a pro-rata calculation of California-attributable contributions relative to total PAC receipts, not just amounts transferred to state accounts.

embedding.summary: FPPC staff counsel advises International Paper Company's PAC on California campaign finance disclosure requirements, including filing deadlines, contribution disclosure thresholds, and the proper method for calculating what portion of multi-state PAC contributions are attributable to California elections.
